title: String Contains Scrambled Pattern
description:
Given two strings pattern and s, return True if s contains a substring that is a permutation of pattern. Use a fixed-size sliding window of length len(pattern) and compare frequency counts.

    

Constraints:
    - 1 ≤ len(pattern) ≤ 10^4
- 1 ≤ len(s) ≤ 10^5
- s and pattern are lowercase English letters
explanation:
Intuition
If a window has the same multiset of characters as pattern, it is a valid permutation. A frequency counter for the current window can be updated in O(1) per step by adding the new character on the right and removing the leftmost character.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Compute counts of pattern and of the first window.
2. Main Algorithm: Slide the window by one character at a time and update counts. If counts match at any step, return True.
3. Return Result: If the scan finishes, return False.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the window grows and shrinks in a
  single pass without revisiting elements more than a constant number of
  times.
- Space Complexity: O(1) to maintain counts, sets, or running
  aggregates needed to validate the window.

Key Insights
- Fixed-size windows allow constant time updates
- Counter equality succinctly encodes permutation equivalence

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to delete zero count keys skews equality checks
- Using sorting per window costs O(n m log m)
hints: Compare character counts over a fixed window
Update by adding one and removing one character
